behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, capped at a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is a pure backoff policy calculation (attempt count/config -> delay). The failure is in local math/limit logic, not in module handoff, OS/network, or external process. Higher levels were considered but rejected because the behavior text only specifies delay progression and cap, with no infrastructure or cross-module dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Test a backoff function/object directly with consecutive failure counts and a max cap; assert exact delay sequence (e.g., 100, 200, 400, ... capped).

behaviors.description: Retry logic recognizes rate-limit responses and computes the correct wait before retrying.
behaviors.justification: The behavior combines response classification (rate-limit detection) with wait-time computation inside retry flow, so module interaction is part of the stated failure boundary. System was rejected: behavior text does not require real HTTP/network; stubbed responses can expose misclassification/handoff bugs. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified because no AI model decisions exist.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry coordinator with a stub transport returning a rate-limit response (e.g., status/headers) and verify it schedules the expected delay for the next attempt.

behaviors.description: After waiting, the client resends the original request and transparently returns the eventual success result.
behaviors.justification: This requires interaction between timer/backoff scheduling, request replay, and response propagation through call(). A unit test of one function would miss replay wiring defects. System was rejected because real network is not required by the behavior text; deterministic in-process transport stubs and fake time can catch this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers plus a stub transport that returns rate-limit first, then success; assert same request payload is resent after delay and call() resolves with the success value.

behaviors.description: When max retries are exhausted, call() returns a typed QuotaExhaustedError (not a generic error).
behaviors.justification: The behavior targets the retry loop boundary exposed through public call(), where retry counting and final error mapping interact. Unit was considered but rejected because testing only an error-constructor helper would not validate the call()-level handoff from retry termination to typed error. System/Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Run call() with a stub transport that always returns rate-limit; configure max retries; assert rejection is QuotaExhaustedError with expected typed fields and not a generic Error.

behaviors.description: The complete retry chain (initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking) terminates and never loops indefinitely before surfacing error.
behaviors.justification: The stated failure mode is cross-component control flow: retry attempts, backoff progression, and quota state must converge to termination. This is beyond a single local function boundary. System was rejected because real network/OS primitives are not required; in-process stubs plus fake timers can reveal non-termination bugs. No AI dependency, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: With fake timers and a consistently rate-limited stub transport, execute call() and assert bounded attempt count, completion within expected virtual time, and final surfaced error (no infinite retry loop).

behaviors.description: Concurrent async callers in one process consume from one shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: This failure appears when multiple callers interact through shared in-process quota state; it is a module/state handoff issue, not single-function logic. System was considered and rejected because behavior text scopes to same-process sharing and does not require real network/OS behavior. No AI model involvement.
behaviors.test_description: Fire multiple concurrent call() invocations against stub transport and shared limiter; assert aggregate allowed attempts follow one shared quota budget rather than per-caller independent budgets.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests wait, dispatch order matches original submission order.
behaviors.justification: This is queueing/dispatcher coordination across pending requests, so the failure boundary is inter-module ordering semantics rather than isolated function math. System was rejected because real network or OS scheduling primitives are not required by the behavior text; controlled in-process async tests can detect FIFO violations. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Queue several call() requests under constrained quota/backoff using controlled async stubs; release capacity stepwise and assert dispatch sequence equals submission sequence.
